topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the glossy patent document, the embossed seal mocking him from the corner of the page. His hands, usually steady when tinkering with circuits, were trembling slightly as he reread the claims. He’d poured three years, nearly all his savings, into the 'Aetheric Resonator,' a device that promised to revolutionize localized energy transfer. Now, the name beside his schematics was already claimed. He pushed the papers away, the sound crisp in the quiet workshop. A tight knot tightened in his chest, pulling at his ribs. He walked over to the workbench, picking up a discarded soldering iron, turning it over and over in his fingers until the metal felt cold and insignificant. "Of course," he muttered, the sound raspy. "Someone else thought of this first." He slumped onto a stool, the weight of the unpatentable idea pressing down on him like a physical weight.

[story 2]

"So, you're telling me," Clara said, her voice dangerously level, tapping a manicured nail against the thick legal paper, "that my design for the kinetic dampener is already secured by someone else?" Her jaw was clenched so tightly a muscle jumped near her temple. She stood up abruptly, the movement sharp and jerky. She paced the length of her studio, her heels clicking a rapid, agitated rhythm on the polished concrete floor. Every glance toward the shelf where her prototype lay felt like a physical prick. A hot flush crept up her neck, staining her cheeks a deep crimson. She stopped by the window, gripping the frame until her knuckles were white. The city lights blurred outside, mirroring the chaotic swirl of frustration inside her.

[story 3]

I watched the lawyer slide the final paperwork across the mahogany desk. The official stamp, the date—it all confirmed it. My breakthrough, the fluid dynamics model I’d spent eighteen months perfecting, was already boxed up. A slow, burning heat began to rise in my throat, making my voice catch. I didn't move for a full minute, just staring at the signature line, my breath coming in shallow, uneven gasps. When I finally spoke, the words were clipped, devoid of their usual enthusiasm. "They didn't wait," I managed, the bitterness thick in my tone. I picked up a heavy paperweight and dropped it onto the desk with a resounding thud that echoed the sudden deflation of my ambition.
